hook: Prompt-only defenses do not make browser agents safe: Operator still showed 23% prompt-injection susceptibility after mitigations, and weak containment enabled a credential/data-exfiltration-style attack in 24 of 25 retries in one Anthropic setup. The architecture proposed here is to make the browser runtime itself the security boundary with fresh non-persistent BrowserContexts, origin-scoped permissions, supervised handoff on sensitive steps, and injection monitoring.
why_it_matters: If you're learning about browser-based LLM agents, this gives you a concrete security model for evaluating whether a web automation system is safe enough to use on untrusted pages.
action: Write a 3-sentence design note that lists the four runtime controls from the article—ephemeral BrowserContext per task, origin-scoped permission grants, sensitive-action handoff, and injection-aware runtime monitoring—and decide which one your current browser-agent setup lacks most.
